mission: I build production systems and document everything I learn along the way. This is my public learning log — projects, questions, and the resources that answered them.

projects.title: FastAPI LMS
projects.desc: Learning‑management backend — enrollment, progress tracking, role‑based access.
learnings: FastAPI dependency injection for authentication
SQLModel relationships and joins
Pytest fixtures for database testing
Async endpoint design patterns
projects.codeSnippet: # main.py — enrollment router from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException from sqlmodel import Session, select router = APIRouter(prefix="/enrollments", tags=["enrollments"]) @router.post("{course_id}") async def enroll_student( course_id: int, student: Student = Depends(get_current_student), session: Session = Depends(get_session), ): existing = session.exec( select(Enrollment).where( Enrollment.course_id == course_id, Enrollment.student_id == student.id, ) ).first() if existing: raise HTTPException(400, "Already enrolled") enrollment = Enrollment(course_id=course_id, student_id=student.id) session.add(enrollment) session.commit() return {"status": "enrolled", "course_id": course_id}

projects.title: AI Digital Card
projects.desc: Production microservice generating dynamic profile cards.
learnings: FastAPI static file serving
Template rendering with Jinja2
Asynchronous profile fetching
Render deployment workflows
projects.codeSnippet: # app.py — card render endpoint from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse app = FastAPI() @app.get("/card/{username}", response_class=HTMLResponse) async def render_card(username: str): profile = await fetch_profile(username) return templates.TemplateResponse( "card.html", {"profile": profile, "theme": profile.theme}, )

projects.title: AI Training Hub
projects.desc: Model‑serving endpoint for a small training‑data platform.
learnings: Saving and loading scikit‑learn models with joblib
Building Pydantic request schemas
Returning prediction probabilities
projects.codeSnippet: # predict.py import joblib from fastapi import APIRouter router = APIRouter() model = joblib.load("models/classifier.pkl") @router.post("/predict") def predict(features: FeaturePayload): x = features.to_array() proba = model.predict_proba([x])[0] return {"label": model.classes_[proba.argmax()], "confidence": round(float(proba.max()), 3)}

questions.q: How do you handle a site that renders content with JavaScript?
questions.a: Start by viewing the page source, not the rendered DOM — if the data you need isn't in the raw HTML, a plain requests + BeautifulSoup call won't see it either. From there the approach splits three ways: check if the page is calling a JSON API you can hit directly (fastest, most stable); use Selenium or Playwright with an explicit wait for the specific element or network state you need, never a fixed sleep; or, if the site is truly SPA-only, render it headlessly and parse the resulting DOM. The interviewer is listening for whether you reach for a headless browser by default (slow, brittle) or only once you've ruled out a lighter option.

questions.q: How do you make a scraper resilient to layout changes?
questions.a: Treat the site's markup as an unstable contract. Select on stable attributes (IDs, data-* attributes, ARIA labels) instead of deep CSS paths or nth-child chains that break the moment a div is inserted upstream. Centralize every selector in one config or constants file so a site redesign means editing one place, not hunting through the codebase. Most importantly, validate the shape of what you scraped (expected fields present, types correct) and fail loudly with a clear error rather than silently returning empty or malformed records — silent breakage is the version of this bug that actually costs money in production.

questions.q: What's the difference between async and sync in FastAPI, and when does it matter?
questions.a: async def hands control back to the event loop the moment your code hits an await on an I/O-bound operation (a DB call, an HTTP request, a file read), so FastAPI can serve other requests while that one waits. Define a route with plain def instead and FastAPI runs it in a worker thread pool so it still doesn't block the loop — but that pool has a finite size, so under heavy concurrent load a sync endpoint doing blocking I/O will eventually queue. The mistake to call out explicitly: writing async def but then calling a blocking (non-async) library inside it. That blocks the entire event loop for every request, which is worse than just using def in the first place.

questions.q: How do you avoid getting an IP banned while scraping at scale?
questions.a: Lead with the constraint that actually matters first: check robots.txt and the site's terms before writing a single line, since some data simply isn't yours to take regardless of technique. Beyond that, rate-limit deliberately (don't hammer a server faster than a human would browse), add jitter to request timing so traffic doesn't look robotic, and rotate sessions/IPs only as a way to spread legitimate load — not to evade a block you've already earned. If a site is actively rate-limiting you, that's a signal to slow down and cache, not a puzzle to route around.

modules.d: Distributions, hypothesis testing, A/B test power analysis.
Cleaning, joins, groupby pipelines, handling missing data.
Neural net basics, embeddings, calling and evaluating LLM APIs.
Serving predictions behind a FastAPI endpoint, monitoring drift.

questions.q: How do you choose between precision and recall for a given problem?
questions.a: Don't lead with a metric — lead with the cost of each error type. In fraud detection, a missed fraud case (false negative) usually costs more than a false alarm (false positive), so you optimize for recall and accept lower precision. In a spam filter, a false positive that buries a real email is worse than letting a spam message through, so precision wins. Say this out loud in the interview: 'it depends on which mistake is more expensive here,' then walk through the specific business context before naming a metric. Only reach for F1 as a single number when the two error costs are genuinely balanced, which is rarer than most candidates assume.

questions.q: What's the difference between bagging and boosting?
questions.a: Both combine many weak models, but they attack different problems. Bagging (e.g. Random Forest) trains models in parallel on independent bootstrap samples of the data and averages them — this reduces variance, so it helps most when your base model overfits. Boosting (e.g. Gradient Boosting, AdaBoost) trains models sequentially, where each new model is trained specifically to correct the errors of the ensemble so far — this reduces bias, so it helps most when your base model underfits. The practical follow-up an interviewer wants: bagging parallelizes trivially and resists overfitting better; boosting is more sample‑efficient but needs more careful tuning (learning rate, early stopping) to avoid overfitting the training data.

questions.q: How do you detect and handle data leakage?
questions.a: Ask one question for every feature: 'would this value actually be available at the moment I need to make the prediction?' If a feature is derived from data that only exists after the outcome happened, or if it was computed using the full dataset (mean, scaler, PCA) before the train/test split, it's leaking. The fix isn't a single trick — it's discipline: fit every preprocessing step only on the training fold, wrap the whole pipeline in one object so it can't be applied out of order, and for time‑series data use a split that respects chronology instead of a random shuffle. A model that looks suspiciously good in cross‑validation is the first clue to check for this.

questions.q: How would you evaluate whether an LLM‑based feature is actually working in production?
questions.a: Resist the urge to trust a few good‑looking demo outputs — that's the trap most teams fall into. Build a small offline evaluation set of real inputs with known‑good expected outputs (or clear grading criteria) and re‑run it on every model or prompt change. Layer in human review sampling for the cases automated metrics can't judge well (tone, correctness of open‑ended answers). Most importantly, tie the feature to a downstream business metric you already track — support ticket resolution time, conversion, retention — because a model can score well on an eval set and still not move the number that actually matters.

modules.d: Growth rates, unit economics, probability for decision‑making.
Designing valid tests, confounders, correlation vs. causation.
Time series basics, cohort analysis, revenue forecasting.
Picking the right chart, avoiding vanity metrics, executive summaries.
Translating a model's output into a decision a stakeholder can act on.

questions.q: A metric moved 20% overnight — how do you investigate?
questions.a: Resist jumping straight to a causal story — most overnight metric swings are instrumentation, not behavior. Check first for pipeline or tracking changes that shipped around the same time: a deploy, a schema change, a tagging update. Next, segment the movement by cohort, channel, device, and geography — a real behavioral shift is usually broad, while a tracking bug is often concentrated in one segment (one browser, one region, one traffic source). Only once those are ruled out do you look for a genuine external cause (a competitor's move, seasonality, a marketing campaign). Naming this order out loud is what separates a senior answer from a junior one.

questions.q: How do you explain statistical significance to a non‑technical stakeholder?
questions.a: Avoid the word 'proof' entirely — frame it as 'how surprising this result would be if nothing had actually changed.' A statistically significant result just means the difference is unlikely to be random noise at your chosen confidence threshold; it says nothing about whether the difference is big enough to matter commercially. Always pair it with practical significance: a 0.1% lift can be statistically significant with enough traffic and still not be worth shipping. And flag the classic trap explicitly — checking results early and stopping the moment they look good inflates the false‑positive rate, so the sample size needs to be decided before the test starts, not adjusted based on how it's trending.
questions.linkLabel: Evan Miller: How Not to Run an A/B Test

questions.q: What makes a dashboard actually get used versus ignored?
questions.a: Start from the decision, not the data. Every chart on the dashboard should map to a question someone actually needs answered before acting — if you can't name that decision, cut the chart. Lean on how people actually perceive charts: position and length (bar and line charts) are read far more accurately at a glance than color or area, so save color for drawing attention to what's abnormal rather than encoding the primary value. Default to the time window the audience actually thinks in (this week, this quarter) instead of whatever the database happened to return, and put the one number that matters most in the top‑left, since that's where eyes land first.
questions.linkLabel: Nielsen Norman Group: Dashboards & preattentive processing

questions.q: How would you forecast revenue for a product with seasonal demand?
questions.a: Start by decomposing the series into trend, seasonality, and residual noise rather than fitting a single curve straight through raw historical revenue — mixing a real seasonal dip with a genuine downward trend leads to bad forecasts either way. Once decomposed, choose a model sized to the data you actually have: a simple moving‑average or exponential‑smoothing model for a short, noisy history; something like SARIMA or Prophet once you have several full seasonal cycles to learn from. Whatever you use, report a range, not a single number — stakeholders making budget decisions need to know how wide the uncertainty is, especially heading into a season you've only seen once or twice before.

testimonials.quote: Rizwan rebuilt our data pipeline and the FastAPI layer on top of it in under three weeks. Nothing has gone down since, and the handoff docs meant our own team could pick it up immediately.
